design_philosophy.core_loop: Single-elimination tournament progression through martial arts matches, building rage gauge to execute powerful Desperation Moves and special attacks strategically
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management of rage gauge across fights; balancing when to spend special moves vs. building meter for super attacks
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Master martial artist competing in a prestigious King of Fighters tournament; revenge narrative against criminal underworld (Geese Howard)
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Emphasizes strategic use of taunts and special attack management through rage gauge system. Retains all 12 playable characters from original roster rather than focusing solely on protagonist. Story-driven character selection with narrative justification for tournament participation. Desperation Moves serve both mechanical and character expression purposes.

difficulty.lives_system: Standard arcade fighting game stock system; loss ends character run
difficulty.checkpointing: Tournament bracket progression; defeating opponents advances player, losses restart from beginning or use continue
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Character roster selection (12 playable characters with varying movesets and effectiveness); player chooses which character to main for single-player run. Bonus stages offer strategic choices to build rage gauge or increase health before critical fights.

progression.upgrade_system: Rage gauge system (similar to spirit system from Art of Fighting 1) - accumulates during combat and can be spent on special attacks. Bonus stages allow players to increase rage gauge maximum or increase character health pool.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Geese Howard available as secret boss if player meets specific requirements during single-player mode. All 12 characters available from start for character selection.
replayability_hooks: 12 different playable characters with unique movesets and playstyles
Character-specific Initiate Super Death Blow stages provide varied bonus stage experiences
Geese Howard secret boss encounter unlocked through specific play conditions
Versus mode for multiplayer competition with full roster (including Geese in SNES version)

world_building_techniques: Character roster serves narrative function: each fighter has canonical reason for tournament participation (Sakazaki family revenge arc, Robert Garcia alliance, newcomers with personal motivations)
Tournament framing device (King of Fighters tournament) provides narrative cohesion

Connection to Fatal Fury universe established through Geese Howard crossover; Art of Fighting 2 positioned as prequel to Fatal Fury timeline (set in 1979, two years before Geese Howard story in Fatal Fury)
Character introductions and endings convey story progression and motivations

boss_design.philosophy: Escalating difficulty with final boss as significant narrative threat (Geese Howard). Tournament structure creates natural boss progression. Mr. Big appears as penultimate encounter before final boss, creating multi-stage ending sequence.
notable_bosses: Geese Howard (secret/final boss) - criminal underworld antagonist, significant enough to warrant crossover from Fatal Fury series
Mr. Big (penultimate boss) - returns from first game as recurring antagonist

faction_indicators: Sakazaki family (Ryo, Yuri, Takuma) - united by revenge narrative against Geese Howard's criminal organization
Robert Garcia - allied with Sakazaki family for tournament
Kisaragi clan ninja tradition - Eiji represents ancient ninja lineage sworn enemy to Kyokugenryu Karate
Kyokugenryu Karate school - martial arts tradition represented by Sakazaki family
danger_signals: Geese Howard as rising criminal underworld figure with reach to manipulate multiple fighters
Mafia presence in South Town - organized crime infrastructure
Mercenary availability - Eiji willing to kill for payment suggests morally ambiguous tournament participants
environmental_storytelling: South Town setting establishes criminal underworld presence and class dynamics
Port location (Temjin's dock worker status) suggests international tournament draw and economic stratification
Bonus stages (tree-chopping, punk-defeating) serve as environmental character expression reflecting martial tradition and urban survival
